analogy: Think of "Sociology and Society" through the chapter's own opening images: market that decides which subject choice may increase or decrease your chances in the job market. The third and fourth advice complicate the matter even more. It is not just our personal effort or just the job market that makes a difference — our gender... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CHAPTER 1, SOCIOLOGY AND SOCIETY, INTRODUCTION, 2 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Sociology and Society is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that market that decides which subject choice may increase or decrease your chances in the job market.

The text explains that the third and fourth advice complicate the matter even more.

The text explains that it is not just our personal effort or just the job market that makes a difference — our gender and family or social background also matter.

The text explains that individual efforts matter a great deal but do not necessarily define outcomes.

The text explains that as we saw there are other social factors that play an important role in the final outcome.

The text explains that here we have only mentioned the ‘job market’, the ‘socioeconomic background’ and ‘gender’.

The text explains that we could well ask, “Who decides what is a ‘good job’?” Do all societies have similar notions of what is a “good job?” Is money the criteria?

The text explains that or is it respect or social recognition or individual satisfaction that decides the worth of a job?

The text explains that do culture and social norms have any role to play?

The text explains that the individual student must study hard to do well.

The text explains that but how well h/she does is structured by a whole set of societal factors.

The text explains that the job market is defined by the needs of the economy.

The text explains that cHAPTER 1 SOCIOLOGY AND SOCIETY I INTRODUCTION Let us begin with some suggestions that are often made to young students like you.

The text explains that one advice often made is, “Study hard and you will do well in life.” The second advice as often made is, “ If you do this subject or set of subjects you will have a better chance of getting a good job in the future”.

The text explains that the third could be, “ As a boy this does not seem a correct choice of subject” or “As a girl, do you think your choice of subjects is a practical one?” The fourth, “Your family needs you to get a job soon so why choose a profession that will take a very long time” or “You will join your family business so why do you wish to do this subject?” Let us examine the suggestions.

The text explains that do you think the first advice contradicts the other three?

The text explains that 2 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY The needs of the economy are again determined by the economic and political policies pursued by the government.

The text explains that this gives us a preliminary sense of how sociology studies human society as an interconnected whole.

The text explains that and how society and the individual interact with each other.

The text explains that the problem of choosing subjects in the senior secondary school is a source of personal worry for the individual student.

The text explains that the social esteem that a particular kind of job has or does not have for an individual depends on the culture of his/her ‘relevant society’.

The text explains that does it mean the ‘society’ the individual belongs to?

The text explains that second, this chapter therefore looks at how the individual in modern times belongs to more than one society.

The text explains that third, this chapter introduces sociology as a systematic study of society, distinct from philosophical and religious reflections, as well as our everyday common sense observation about society.

The text explains that fourth, this distinct way of studying society can be better understood if we look back historically at the intellectual ideas and material contexts within which sociology was born and later grew.

The text explains that understanding the history of a discipline helps understand the discipline.

The text explains that iI TH E SO C I O L O G I C A L IM A G I N A T I O N : THE PERSONAL PROBLEM AND THE PUBLIC ISSUE We began with a set of suggestions that drew our attention to how the individual and society are dialectically linked.

The text explains that wright Mills rests his vision of the sociological imagination precisely in the unravelling of how the personal and public are related.

The text explains that 3 SOCIOLOGY AND SOCIETY The sociological imagination enables us to grasp history and biography and the relations between the two within society.

The text explains that that is its task and promise… Perhaps the most fruitful distinction with which the sociological imagination works is between ‘the personal troubles of the milieu’ and ‘the public issues of social structure’...

The text explains that troubles occur within the character of the individual and within the range of his immediate relations with others; they have to do with his self and with those limited areas of social life of which he is directly and personally aware...

The text explains that the facts of contemporary history are also facts about the success and the failure of individual men and women.

The text explains that when a society is industrialised, a peasant becomes a worker; a feudal lord is liquidated or becomes a businessman.

The text explains that when wars happen, an insurance salesman becomes a rocket launcher; a store clerk, a radar man; a wife lives alone; a child grows up without a father.

The text explains that neither the life of an individual nor the history of a society can be understood without understanding both...

The text explains that the sociological imagination helps to understand and explain homelessness as a public issue.

The text explains that 4 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY This question of what to focus in society is indeed central to sociology.

The text explains that iII PLURALITIES AND INEQUALITIES AMONG SOCIETIES In the contemporary world we belong, in a sense, to more than one ‘society’.

The text explains that when amidst foreigners reference to ‘our society’ may mean ‘Indian society’, but when amongst fellow Indians we may use the term ‘our society’ to denote a linguistic or ethnic community, a religious or caste or tribal society.

The text explains that this diversity makes deciding which ‘society’ we are talking about difficult.

The text explains that but perhaps this difficulty of mapping society is not confined to sociologists alone as the comment below will show.

The text explains that or would you rather go back in t i m e - w a y b a c k t o t h e E p i c s , where the gods and demons took sides in the great battle where b r o t h e r s k i l l e d b r o t h e r s … Or would you rather stay where you are, right in the present, in the heart of this monstrous, teeming, bewildering city, and try to orchestrate its dizzying contrasts of sight and sound and milieu?

The text explains that 5 SOCIOLOGY AND SOCIETY Discuss the visuals What kind of pluralities and inequalities do they show?

The text explains that 6 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY everyday life and also about others’ lives, about our own ‘society’ and also about others’ ‘society’.

The text explains that however the observations and ideas that sociology as a discipline makes about ‘society’ is different from both that of philosophical reflections and common sense.

The text explains that observations of philosophical and religious thinkers are often about what is moral or immoral in human behaviour, about the desirable way of living and about a good society.

The text explains that (In Chapter 3, you will see how sociology of religion is different from a theological study).

The text explains that it only means that when a sociologist studies a society, the sociologist is willing to observe and collect findings, even if they are not to her/his personal liking.

The text explains that iV INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY You have already been acquainted with the sociological imagination and the central concern of sociology to study society as an interconnected whole.

The text explains that discuss how there is greater give and take among disciplines today.

The text explains that identify any personal problem that you or your friends or relatives are facing.

The text explains that 23 SOCIOLOGY AND SOCIETY READINGS BERGER , PETER L.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology, Chapter 1: Sociology and Society

prelims_traps: Do not treat CHAPTER 1 and SOCIOLOGY AND SOCIETY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Sociology and Society; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Sociology and Society through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CHAPTER 1 and SOCIOLOGY AND SOCIETY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
